title: Design Docs & RFCs for SAP Architects
description: How FAANG lives and dies by design documents. Anatomy of a FAANG design doc, writing for executive audience, design doc review culture, real SAP design doc examples, decision logs.

sections.title: Anatomy of a FAANG Design Doc

description.value:
At FAANG, the design document is the atomic unit of technical decision-making. Nothing of significance gets built without a written design doc that is reviewed, criticized, and approved by peers. This culture is foreign to most SAP architects, who are used to functional specifications (FS) and technical specifications (TS) that describe configuration steps rather than engineering tradeoffs.

A FAANG design doc typically starts as a Google Doc (or Coda/Notion) with a specific template. The header includes the author, reviewers, date, and status (Draft → Review → Approved → Obsolete). The document is living — it evolves as the design matures. The first section is always the Problem Statement: what problem are we solving, for whom, and why now? This is followed by Context & Background: what systems exist, what decisions have been made, what constraints are we operating under?

The heart of the document is the Proposed Design section. This describes the architecture at multiple levels of abstraction: system context diagram (C4 Level 1), container diagram (Level 2), component diagram (Level 3), and key interactions. FAANG designers use C4 notation or UML sequence diagrams, not Visio wireframes. Every component is named, every data flow is annotated, every API is specified (request/response schema, error codes, pagination).

The Alternatives Considered section is what separates Staff+ engineers from juniors. You must show that you explored at least 2–3 alternatives before converging on your design. For each alternative, describe: the approach, why it was rejected (cost, complexity, scalability limitations), and what tradeoff you made. FAANG interviewers scrutinize this section most — if you can't articulate why you didn't choose Kafka over Pub/Sub, you haven't done the analysis.

The document concludes with Security & Privacy Review (data classification, encryption, access control), Operational Excellence (monitoring, alerting, runbooks, SLIs/SLOs), and Open Questions (known unknowns that need further investigation). Every design doc must have an executive summary at the top — because VPs don't read past 200 words.

Context & Background — Existing systems, constraints</text><rect class="doc-header" x="30" y="165" width="640" height="30"/><text class="doc-label" x="350" y="185">3. # Design Doc Header Template
# Author: [Name]
# Reviewers: [Peer1, Peer2, TL, Architect]
# Status: Draft | In Review | Approved | Obsolete
# Last Updated: [Date]

# Problem Statement
#   What: [Single sentence describing the problem]
#   Who: [Stakeholders affected]
#   Why Now: [Business/technical urgency]

# Context & Background
#   - Current system architecture
#   - Prior decisions and their rationale
#   - Constraints (compliance, budget, timeline)

# Proposed Design
#   - C4 Level 1: System Context
#   - C4 Level 2: Container Diagram
#   - C4 Level 3: Component Diagram
#   - Key interactions (sequence diagrams)
#   - API specifications (request/response)

# Alternatives Considered
#   | Alternative | Pros | Cons | Verdict |
#   |-------------|------|------|---------|
#   | Approach A  | ...  | ...  | Rejected |
#   | Approach B  | ...  | ...  | Selected  |

# Security Review
#   - Data classification: PII/PHI/Internal
#   - Encryption at rest and in transit
#   - Access control model (RBAC/ABAC)

decisionTree.title: Choosing the Right Design Doc Structure for Your Audience
decisionTree.prompt: Should I write a FAANG-style design doc, a lightweight ADR, or a traditional SAP functional specification for this initiative?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Who is the primary audience and what decision needs to be made?" If the audience is peer engineers and you need to make an architectural decision with tradeoffs analyzed, use a FAANG-style design doc. If the audience is yourself or your immediate team and you need to capture a single decision's rationale, use an ADR. If the audience is external consultants or auditors and you need contractual specification, use an SAP functional spec.

branches.condition: Peer engineers need to review, criticize, and approve an architectural decision with multiple alternatives
branches.path: FAANG-style design doc (3-5 pages). Headers: Problem Statement, Context, Proposed Design, Alternatives Considered, Security Review. Status: Draft → Review → Approved. Reviewers: domain expert + cross-team rep + Staff engineer. Must include C4 diagrams and explicit tradeoffs.

branches.condition: Need to capture a single architectural decision and its rationale for future reference (institutional memory)
branches.path: ADR (1-2 pages). Format: ADR-NNNN-title.md. Sections: Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Compliance. Store in repo under docs/adr/. Immutable — if decision changes, write a new ADR superseding the old one.

branches.condition: External vendor, auditor, or regulatory body needs a contractual specification of system behavior
branches.path: SAP functional specification / technical specification. Detailed step-by-step configuration. Include screenshots, transaction codes, IMG paths. Focus on traceability to requirements. Use SAP Activate methodology templates.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The Staff+ answer: know when to use which. Never write a 5-page design doc for a decision that could be an ADR. Never write an ADR when you need peer review. The design doc is for exploring tradeoffs; the ADR is for recording decisions. Most SAP teams over-invest in FSDs and under-invest in design docs and ADRs.

description.value:
FAANG Staff+ engineers spend as much time writing for executives as they do writing code. A design doc that isn't read is a design doc that doesn't matter. Executives (Directors, VPs, CTOs) have a 30-second attention span for technical details. Your design doc must communicate the essence before they scroll.

The Executive Summary is the most important paragraph you will write. It must answer four questions: (1) What are we proposing to build? (2) Why does it matter to the business? (3) How much will it cost (engineering months, infrastructure dollars)? (4) What is the risk of doing nothing? A strong executive summary can be written in 3–5 sentences. Every word must carry weight — eliminate jargon, avoid SAP-specific acronyms, and state the business outcome first.

Decision-Ready Documents: Executives want to make decisions, not learn about architecture. Format your doc so the decision is obvious. Use a Recommendation Callout Box at the top: "Recommendation: Adopt Option B (event-driven architecture with Kafka) by Q3 2026, costing 6 engineering months and $15K/mo in infrastructure." The recommendation must be explicit, concrete, and time-bound.

Visual Hierarchy: Use diagrams that executives can grok in 5 seconds. The C4 System Context diagram is ideal — it shows the system as a box, users as stick figures, and dependencies as arrows with labels. Avoid UML class diagrams, ERDs with 50 tables, or anything that requires zooming. Use color strategically: green for existing systems, blue for proposed systems, red for deprecated systems.

The TL;DR Culture: FAANG executives expect a TL;DR (Too Long; Didn't Read) section at the very top. This is 2–3 bullet points that capture the essence. Below the TL;DR, include a table of contents with links to sections. And never hide bad news — if the migration will break existing APIs, state it plainly. Executives trust engineers who surface risks early.

organizationalDimension.title: Adopting Executive-Facing Design Docs in an SAP Organization
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Start by writing one executive summary each quarter for a major platform initiative. Use the 3-box framework (Where We Are → Target State → How We Get There). Get feedback from a VP-level sponsor. Once the format is proven, create a template approved by the CTO and roll it out to all Staff+ engineers. Require executive summaries for any initiative above $250K.

stakeholders.name: VP of Engineering
stakeholders.concern: Docs must be decision-ready, not informational. Need explicit recommendation and cost estimate upfront.
stakeholders.engagement: Co-create first template. Review first 3 docs personally. Approve template for org-wide use.

stakeholders.name: Staff+ Engineers
stakeholders.concern: Executive summaries add overhead. They're used to writing detailed technical specs, not concise business cases.
stakeholders.engagement: Provide writing workshops. Create a peer review process for executive summaries. Reward well-written executive summaries in performance reviews.

stakeholders.name: Product Managers
stakeholders.concern: Technical executive summaries may conflict with product roadmaps not yet communicated to leadership
stakeholders.engagement: Align executive summary timing with quarterly planning cycles. Pre-brief PMs before docs go to executives.

activities: Design executive summary template with TL;DR, Recommendation, Cost, Risk sections
Write 2 example docs from past initiatives
Train 10 Staff+ engineers on executive writing
Each writes one executive summary for their current initiative
Publish template and guidelines to internal wiki
Make executive summary mandatory for Architecture Review Board submissions

metrics: Executive summary read rate (target: > 80% of docs read by VP+ within 1 week)
Decision turnaround time (target: < 2 weeks from submission to decision)
Staff+ engineer satisfaction with writing process (target: > 4.0/5.0)
antiPatterns: Writing executive summaries that are just shorter versions of technical specs — must stand alone with business context
Hiding bad news or risks — executives trust engineers who are transparent about challenges
Using SAP jargon (ECC, S/4HANA, BTP, RFC) without explanation — executives from non-SAP backgrounds won't understand

description.value:
FAANG's design review culture is adversarial by design — not personally adversarial, but intellectually rigorous. Reviewers are expected to find flaws, ask hard questions, and reject designs that don't meet the bar. This is not SAP's "sign-off" culture where approvers rubber-stamp functional specs. At FAANG, a design review is a crucible that makes the design stronger.

The Reviewer Allocation is deliberate. You need at least 3 reviewers: a domain expert who understands the technical area deeply, a cross-team representative who ensures the design doesn't harm neighboring systems, and a senior engineer (Staff+) who evaluates architectural soundness. Each reviewer has 48–72 hours to provide written feedback. The author responds to each comment in-thread, and open issues must be resolved before the doc can transition from "In Review" to "Approved."

Types of Feedback: FAANG culture distinguishes between blocking and non-blocking feedback. Blocking feedback means the design cannot proceed until this issue is resolved — typically security vulnerabilities, data loss risks, or fundamental architectural flaws. Non-blocking feedback includes style suggestions, alternative approaches that are equally valid, or questions about future extensibility. The author decides which feedback to incorporate and which to defer with explicit rationale.

The Design Review Meeting (DRM) is a 60-minute synchronous session where the design is presented, questioned, and either approved or sent back for revision. The meeting starts with a 15-minute presentation by the author (assume everyone has read the doc — if they haven't, reschedule). Then 35 minutes of Q&A. The last 10 minutes are for the decision: Approve, Approve with Conditions, or Reject. Rejected designs need a clear explanation of what must change for re-submission.

SAP teams adopting this culture should start with lightweight RFCs for architecture decisions, then graduate to full design docs. The transition is hard — SAP engineers are used to "requirements in → solution out" without peer scrutiny. But the result is dramatically better architecture, fewer production incidents, and faster onboarding of new team members who can read design docs to understand the system.

adversarialResponse.title: When Design Review Culture Meets SAP Project Governance
adversarialResponse.challenge: You introduce a FAANG-style design review process to your SAP team: every significant technical decision requires a written design doc reviewed by 3 peer engineers before implementation. The team resists: 'We've never needed this before. We deliver on time without it. This is just bureaucracy slowing us down.' After 2 months, only 2 design docs have been written. Engineers are bypassing the process by making decisions in Jira comments and Slack threads. The project manager complains that design reviews are delaying the delivery timeline.
adversarialResponse.badResponse: Escalate to the Engineering Director and mandate design docs as a gate. Everyone writes docs grudgingly, but they're low quality: copied from previous projects, no real alternatives considered, rubber-stamped by reviewers. The process exists on paper but provides zero value. After 6 months, the Director abandons the requirement, citing overhead. The team feels vindicated: 'See? We told you it was useless.'
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: Pivot the approach: (1) Reduce the initial bar — start with lightweight 1-page design docs instead of 5-page documents. A design doc can be a Google Doc with just Problem, Approach, and Why Not Alternatives. (2) Identify 2-3 high-visibility, high-risk decisions (e.g., Event Mesh vs PI/PO for a new integration) and require full design docs only for these. Success on these high-risk decisions proves the value. (3) After 3 successful high-risk design docs, socialize the results: 'The Event Mesh design doc caught a scalability issue that would have cost $500K in rework.' (4) Gradually expand the scope: medium-risk decisions next quarter, all significant decisions the quarter after. (5) Keep the lightweight option for low-risk decisions. Result: 12 months later, design docs are the default for all significant decisions. The team now insists on them because they've personally experienced the value.
adversarialResponse.pattern: The Trojan Horse pattern: start with a lightweight version that delivers immediate value on high-risk items, then expand scope based on demonstrated success. This is the same pattern used by Google's Site Reliability Culture adoption at non-Google companies, and by Spotify's Squad Model adoption across enterprises.

description.value:
Translating the FAANG design doc culture to SAP requires real examples that SAP architects recognize. Here are three design doc scenarios that SAP Staff+ candidates should practice writing.

Scenario 1: Migration from ECC to S/4HANA with Selective Data Transition. The design doc must cover: 200+ custom Z-programs to assess for compatibility, 50+ interfaces to migrate from PI/PO to BTP Integration Suite, data volume analysis (3 TB of financial data), downtime window of 48 hours maximum (hard constraint from business due to continuous operations), and rollback strategy if S/4 doesn't meet performance SLAs. The alternatives considered section compares brownfield (system conversion), greenfield (new implementation), and selective data transition (mix). The recommendation must be backed by cost estimates (licensing, infrastructure, consulting) and risk analysis (data loss probability, cutover complexity).

Scenario 2: Real-Time Reporting Platform Using CDS + HANA + Fiori. This doc addresses latency requirements: financial closing reports must load in under 3 seconds (P95), operational reports can tolerate 10 seconds. The design proposes HANA Calculation Views with OLAP-style aggregations, CDS Views with @Analytics annotations, and Fiori Elements with SmartTable. Alternatives include SAP BW/4HANA (too heavy, 6-month implementation) and direct table reads (too slow for aggregation queries). The tradeoff analysis compares query complexity vs. maintenance burden.

Scenario 3: Event-Driven Integration Hub Using SAP BTP Event Mesh. The problem is 500+ interfaces running on legacy PI/PO with 30% failure rate and no observability. The design proposes Event Mesh as the backbone with CloudEvents standard, schema registry for IDoc type evolution, and Kafka Connect for sink/source connectors. The alternatives section compares Event Mesh vs. Kafka on Confluent Cloud vs. IBM MQ. The tradeoffs cover operational overhead, cost per message, and integration depth with SAP systems. This design doc should include a migration plan with parallel run strategy.

Each of these scenarios should be written as a real design doc with all the sections described in Section 1. Practice writing these under a 2-hour timebox — the same constraint you'll face in a FAANG system design interview.

CloudEvents + Schema Registry</text><text class="sap-dd-sub" x="350" y="322">Alternatives: Confluent Kafka vs IBM MQ vs Event Mesh</text></svg>

# Design Doc: ECC to S/4HANA Selective Data Transition
# Status: Draft
# Author: [Staff Engineer]
# Reviewers: [SAP Architect, Infra Lead, Security Lead]

# TL;DR
# - Migrate 3 TB of financial data from ECC 6.0 to S/4HANA 2023
# - Selective data transition approach: 80% greenfield, 20% brownfield
# - 48h cutover window with parallel run for 4 weeks
# - Rollback: failover to ECC read-only within 2 hours

# Problem Statement
# SAP ECC 6.0 mainstream maintenance ends Dec 2027.
# We operate 200+ custom programs, 50+ interfaces, 3 TB of data.
# Financial close requires <4h window (currently 8h in ECC).

# Context
# - ECC on HANA (SoH): 6 application servers, 3 TB HANA DB
# - 50 interfaces: 30 IDoc (ALE), 15 SOAP, 5 REST
# - Compliance: SOX, GDPR (data residency in EU/US)

# Proposed Design
# - Phase 1: Assessment (2 months) - compatibility check
# - Phase 2: Build (6 months) - S/4 with selective data
# - Phase 3: Cutover (48h) - delta sync, switch DNS
# - Phase 4: Hypercare (4 weeks) - parallel run

# Alternatives Considered
# | Approach | Cost | Risk | Duration |
# |----------|------|------|----------|
# | Brownfield | Low | High (data quality) | 4 mo |
# | Greenfield | High | Low | 12 mo |
# | Selective (chosen) | Medium | Medium | 8 mo |

An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) is a short document (1–2 pages) that captures a single architectural decision and its rationale. Unlike a full design doc, an ADR is lightweight, immutable, and searchable. FAANG teams maintain decision logs (often in the repo as docs/adr/NNNN-title.md) that serve as the institutional memory of why the system is the way it is.

The ADR format popularized by Michael Nygard has five sections: Title (ADR-001: Use Event Mesh over PI/PO for New Integrations), Context (the problem and constraints that forced the decision), Decision (a clear statement of what was decided, in active voice: "We will use SAP BTP Event Mesh as the primary integration backbone for all new interfaces"), Consequences (both positive and negative), and Status (Proposed → Accepted → Deprecated → Superseded). Some teams add a Compliance section that tracks whether the decision is being followed.

Why ADRs Matter for SAP Architects: SAP landscapes are notorious for undocumented decisions. "Why is this BADI filtered on company code X?" "Why is this RFC configured with synchronous communication instead of qRFC?" These questions go unanswered when the original architect leaves. ADRs solve this by making decisions explicit and attributing them to a point in time with specific context. When a new architect joins the team, they can read the ADR log and understand the history of architectural decisions in 2 hours instead of 2 months.

ADR Anti-Patterns: The most common mistake is making ADRs too long — if it exceeds 2 pages, it should be a design doc, not an ADR. Another anti-pattern is retroactive ADRs (writing the decision after the code is already written without capturing the actual context). ADRs should be written before implementation begins. A third anti-pattern is ADR sprawl — teams that create ADRs for trivial decisions ("ADR-050: Use tabs instead of spaces"). Reserve ADRs for decisions that have significant architectural impact, are irreversible or expensive to reverse, or affect multiple teams.

For SAP architects interviewing at FAANG, demonstrating familiarity with ADRs is a strong signal. When the interviewer asks "How do you document architecture decisions?", describing the ADR format and giving a concrete SAP example (like the Event Mesh decision above) shows you understand how FAANG maintains technical decision hygiene at scale.

legacy PI/PO interfaces migrated over 12 months</text><rect class="adr-section" x="30" y="192" width="310" height="60" stroke="var(--svg-success-stroke)"/><text class="adr-sub" x="185" y="212">Positive Consequences</text><text class="adr-sub" x="185" y="228">- Observability via Grafana dashboards</text><text class="adr-sub" x="185" y="242">- CloudEvents standard for interoperability</text><rect class="adr-section" x="360" y="192" width="310" height="60" stroke="var(--svg-error-stroke)"/><text class="adr-sub" x="515" y="212">Negative Consequences</text><text class="adr-sub" x="515" y="228">- Migration cost: 6 engineering months</text><text class="adr-sub" x="515" y="242">- Team retraining on Event Mesh APIs</text><rect class="adr-section" x="30" y="265" width="640" height="36"/><text class="adr-sub" x="350" y="280">Status: Accepted | Date: 2026-01-15 | Review Date: 2026-07-15</text><rect class="adr-status" x="30" y="312" width="120" height="24"/><text class="adr-sub" x="90" y="329">Proposed</text><rect class="adr-status" x="160" y="312" width="120" height="24" stroke="var(--svg-success-stroke)"/><text class="adr-sub" x="220" y="329">Accepted</text><rect class="adr-status" x="290" y="312" width="120" height="24" stroke="var(--svg-warning-stroke)"/><text class="adr-sub" x="350" y="329">Deprecated</text><rect class="adr-status" x="420" y="312" width="120" height="24" stroke="var(--svg-error-stroke)"/><text class="adr-sub" x="480" y="329">Superseded</text></svg>

# ADR Template (Markdown)
# docs/adr/0001-use-event-mesh.md

# Title
ADR-001: Use SAP BTP Event Mesh as Primary Integration Backbone

# Status
Accepted (2026-01-15)

# Context
- 500+ legacy PI/PO interfaces with 30% failure rate
- No centralized observability for integration flows
- Growing demand for real-time (vs batch) integrations
- CloudEvents standard adoption for interoperability

# Decision
We will use SAP BTP Event Mesh (backed by Kafka) as the primary
integration backbone for all new interfaces. Legacy PI/PO interfaces
will be migrated over 12 months with parallel run capability.

# Consequences
Positive:
- Native CloudEvents support
- Grafana dashboards for consumer lag, throughput
- Exactly-once semantics for financial transactions

Negative:
- 6 engineering months migration effort
- Team re-training on Event Mesh concepts
- New runtime cost ($5K/mo estimated)

# Compliance
- All new integrations MUST use Event Mesh from Q2 2026
- Legacy interfaces exempt until migration quarter assigned
- Quarterly compliance review by Architecture Review Board

quantifiedTradeoff.title: ADR Investment vs Institutional Knowledge Loss

variables.name: N_engineers
variables.description: Number of engineers in the SAP platform team

variables.name: N_decisions
variables.description: Number of architectural decisions per quarter

variables.name: P_recall
variables.description: Probability an engineer can recall a decision made 6 months ago without documentation

variables.name: C_rework
variables.description: Cost of re-litigating or undoing a bad decision due to forgotten rationale (hours)

variables.name: T_onboard
variables.description: Time for a new engineer to understand architecture without ADRs (weeks)

variables.name: T_onboard_adr
variables.description: Time for a new engineer to understand architecture with ADRs (weeks)

variables.name: N_new_hires
variables.description: Number of new hires per year

calculation.formula: ADR cost = N_decisions × T_adr × N_engineers (reviewers) × 4 quarters. Knowledge loss cost = N_decisions × (1 - P_recall) × C_rework × 4 quarters. Onboarding savings = (T_onboard - T_onboard_adr) × C_engineer_week × N_new_hires.
calculation.cp: ADR cost: 30 × 2 × 3 reviewers × 4 = 720 hours/year. At $125/hr blended: $90,000/year.
calculation.ap: Knowledge loss cost without ADRs: 30 × 0.75 × 40 × 4 = 3,600 hours/year ($450,000). Plus onboarding: (12 - 4) × $5,000 × 6 = $240,000. Total without ADRs: $690,000/year.
calculation.breakeven: ADR investment ($90K) saves $600K/year in rework and onboarding. ROI: 567%. Breakeven: even if ADRs take 4× longer to write, they still pay for themselves. The intangible value (team confidence, faster decision-making) is incalculable.

details: Staff+ Insight: FAANG interviewers evaluate your ability to structure thinking on paper. If you can't write a clear design doc, you can't lead multi-quarter projects. Practice writing 2–3 SAP design docs using the FAANG template before your interview. The ability to communicate complex architecture decisions concisely is the #1 differentiator between Staff and Senior engineers.
